title: DC conductivity of tilted Dirac Fermions across the Lifshitz Transition: short- versus long-range impurities
authors: Mohammad H. Pakzamir, Zahra Faraei, Ali G. Moghaddam
abstract: We theoretically investigate the DC conductivity of two-dimensional tilted Dirac systems subject to short- and long-range impurity scattering. Using the Kubo formalism, we systematically study transport across the subcritical (Type I), critical, and overcritical (Type II) tilt regimes. In the subcritical phase, short-range impurities yield a frequency-independent conductivity that decreases monotonically with tilt. Conversely, long-range Coulomb scattering results in a strongly energy-dependent conductivity governed by a tilt-independent scattering rate. At the Lifshitz transition ($t = 1$), the transport signatures of these impurities diverge fundamentally: the van Hove singularity in the density of states induces a localized conductivity dip for short-range disorder, but a pronounced macroscopic peak for Coulomb impurities. In the overcritical regime, an ultraviolet momentum cutoff is required to regularize the open Fermi surface, leading to distinct behaviors for each impurity type. Notably, the conductivity perpendicular to the tilt direction ($σ_{xx}$) exhibits a cutoff-dependent, non-monotonic peak near $t = \sqrt{2}$ for short-range defects, while it decays monotonically with increasing tilt for long-range scattering. For both potentials, the conductivity along the tilt axis ($σ_{yy}$) increases without bound, revealing extreme transport anisotropy. For long-range impurities, the energy dependence of the conductivity becomes nearly quadratic and linear for Type I and II, respectively. Furthermore, vertex corrections vanish identically at the Lifshitz transition for both impurity types. Finally, we provide a unified geometric framework for these phenomena, establishing the tilt parameter as a powerful knob for engineering macroscopic transport in Dirac materials.
summary: This paper theoretically examines the DC conductivity of 2D tilted Dirac fermions as they pass through a Lifshitz transition point. By comparing short-range and long-range impurity scattering, the authors show that the critical point yields distinct signatures—a dip for short-range scatterers versus a peak for Coulomb impurities. The tilt parameter $t$ is established as a key geometric control knob for engineering transport anisotropy in these topological materials.
main_problem: The study investigates how the DC conductivity of two-dimensional tilted Dirac fermions changes when subjected to short-range versus long-range impurity scattering across a Lifshitz transition.
main_result: The transport signatures diverge fundamentally at the critical point ($t=1$): short-range disorder causes a localized dip, while Coulomb impurities cause a macroscopic peak. The tilt parameter acts as a powerful knob for engineering anisotropy.
method: The analysis employs the Kubo formalism within the first-order Born approximation to calculate the conductivity tensor ($\sigma_{xx}, \sigma_{yy}$), paying close attention to vertex corrections.
model_or_system: The system is modeled by 2D tilted Dirac fermions, whose low-energy excitations are governed by a continuum Hamiltonian dependent on the tilt parameter $t$. The physics is analyzed across three regimes: subcritical ($t<1$), critical ($t=1$), and overcritical ($t>1$).
key_observables: DC conductivity tensor ($\sigma_{xx}, \sigma_{yy}$), scattering self-energy, vertex correction factor.
important_parameters: Tilt parameter $t$, impurity range (short vs. long/Coulomb), Fermi energy ($\epsilon_F$), and momentum cutoff ($\Lambda$).
main_assumptions: The calculations rely on the first-order Born approximation for disorder effects, and regularization via an ultraviolet momentum cutoff ($\Lambda$) is necessary in the overcritical regime.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the evolution of Fermi surface topology across $t=1$, showing $\sigma_{xx}$ vs. $t$ plots that reveal monotonic changes, dips at $t=1$, and non-monotonic peaks in the overcritical regime for different impurity types.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the conductivity using geometric reformulations of the Hamiltonian, contrasting the mathematical behavior (e.g., vertex corrections) and physical outcomes ($\sigma_{xx}$ vs $\sigma_{yy}$) for short-range versus long-range impurities across the three topological regimes defined by $t$.
why_interesting: The work provides a detailed theoretical framework showing how subtle changes in band structure topology (the Lifshitz transition) can lead to dramatically different, predictable macroscopic transport properties based on the nature of disorder.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic evolution of the Fermi surface topology in a tilted Dirac semimetal at a constant energy ω > 0. The contours represent the subcritical closed ellipse (t < 1, blue), the critical Lifshitz transition point (t = 1, green), and the overcritical open hyperbolas (t > 1, red). . The dashed lines illustrate the transverse momentum cutoff Λ required to reg- ularize transport in the overcritical regime.

FIG. 3. Subcritical longitudinal conductivity (t < 1) for short-range impurities as a function of the tilt parameter t. The curves are plotted for different values of the dimension- less impurity scattering strength γimp = 0.05, 0.1, and 0.2, demonstrating a monotonic decrease of the macroscopic con- ductivity as the system approaches the Lifshitz transition.
